title: Exodus 35
subtitle: Sabbath Restated; the Offering; Bezalel and Oholiab Announced

sections.header: Verses 1–19 — Sabbath Restated; the Offering and Skills Announced

notes.note: MacArthur: The seventh day shall be your holy day. The Sabbath command is given again in the context of tabernacle construction -- even the building of the sanctuary must not override the covenant sign.

MacArthur: All who are skilled among you are to come and make everything the LORD has commanded. The tabernacle's construction requires every skilled person to contribute -- the universal mobilisation of gifts for God's house.

heb.paragraph: V.5,21-22: "Everyone who is willing (nĕdîb leb) is to bring to the LORD an offering." The Tabernacle is built entirely by freewill offering — no taxation, no coercion. The word nādîb (generous, willing, noble) is used of those whose hearts were moved (nĕśāʾô libbô — lit. "their hearts lifted them"). The outpouring of generosity described in vv.21-29 — everyone brought more than was needed (36:6-7) — models the principle of 2 Cor 9:7.

V.10,25: "All who are skilled among you are to come and make everything the LORD has commanded." The phrase ḥakam leb ("wise/skilled of heart") appears repeatedly in the Tabernacle construction narrative. In Hebrew thought, skill resides in the heart, not the hands: craftsmanship is a matter of inner formation, not merely technical training. The wise heart is the Spirit-filled heart (31:3-4) that translates vision into realized form.

hist.historical: The voluntary offering (tĕrûmâ) for the tabernacle contrasts with compulsory taxation in Egyptian and Mesopotamian temple economies. The text emphasises willing hearts — 'everyone who was willing' (v. 5) — seven times in this chapter. The materials listed (gold, silver, bronze, dyed fabrics, leather, acacia wood, gemstones, spices, oil) represent the full range of luxury goods available in the Late Bronze Age eastern Mediterranean trade network.
hist.context: The Sabbath command (vv.1–3) comes before the work begins — a deliberate counterweight to the urgency of the project. Even the most sacred building project must stop on the seventh day. The specific prohibition against lighting fire on the Sabbath (v.3) may address the smelting and metalwork required for Tabernacle construction — even holy work respects God's rest pattern.

refs.note: The offering call here mirrors the original instruction in chapter 25: 'from everyone whose heart moves them.' The repetition after the golden calf crisis shows that generosity survives apostasy — the people's hearts are still willing.

The skilled workers named here were first identified in 31:1–11 before the golden calf interruption. Chapters 35–40 resume the building programme, confirming that God's purposes survive human failure.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Sarna notes that nadiv lev (willing heart) is the sole qualification for tabernacle contribution — not wealth, tribal standing, or priestly lineage. The voluntary principle of the offering matches the voluntary principle of the covenant: God invites, never coerces.

The offering list reverses ch.32: the same materials (gold, linen, yarn) that funded the golden calf now fund the tabernacle. Alter notes the literary reversal is deliberate — the community's giving is repentance expressed in the same materials as the sin.

Calvin: 'God requires a willing mind in those who offer their gifts, for he is not allured by wealth but looks at the disposition.' The voluntary offering is the model of all Christian giving — not compelled by law but freely given from a heart transformed by grace.

The NET note on the offering of gold objects — clasps, earrings, rings, necklaces — notes these were personal jewellery of both men and women. The note also observes the contrast with 32:2–3 where the same earrings funded the golden calf — the same personal jewellery redirected from apostasy to worship.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The offering list reverses ch.32: the same materials (gold, linen, yarn) that funded the golden calf now fund the tabernacle. Alter notes the literary reversal is deliberate — the community's giving is repentance expressed in the same materials as the sin.

Calvin: 'God requires a willing mind in those who offer their gifts, for he is not allured by wealth but looks at the disposition.' The voluntary offering is the model of all Christian giving — not compelled by law but freely given from a heart transformed by grace.

The NET note on the offering of gold objects — clasps, earrings, rings, necklaces — notes these were personal jewellery of both men and women. The note also observes the contrast with 32:2–3 where the same earrings funded the golden calf — the same personal jewellery redirected from apostasy to worship.

Calvin: 'God requires a willing mind in those who offer their gifts, for he is not allured by wealth but looks at the disposition.' The voluntary offering is the model of all Christian giving — not compelled by law but freely given from a heart transformed by grace.

The NET note on the offering of gold objects — clasps, earrings, rings, necklaces — notes these were personal jewellery of both men and women. The note also observes the contrast with 32:2–3 where the same earrings funded the golden calf — the same personal jewellery redirected from apostasy to worship.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the offering of gold objects — clasps, earrings, rings, necklaces — notes these were personal jewellery of both men and women. The note also observes the contrast with 32:2–3 where the same earrings funded the golden calf — the same personal jewellery redirected from apostasy to worship.

The NET note on the detailed materials list — clasps, earrings, rings, necklaces, all articles of gold — observes that these were the materials that earlier went into the golden calf. The irony is structural: the same raw material that funded Israel's greatest failure now funds the legitimate worship structure. Repentance redirects rather than replaces.

MacArthur: Everyone who was willing and whose heart moved them came and brought an offering to the LORD. The tabernacle is financed by voluntary generosity, not mandatory taxation. When grace is truly understood, generosity overflows.

MacArthur: The voluntary freewill offering is the economic foundation of the tabernacle. Every material used in the sanctuary came from the same community that had just sinned catastrophically with the golden calf. The offering is simultaneously repentance, restoration, and worship — the community redirects its gold from the calf to the covenant dwelling.

heb.paragraph: V.21-29: The tĕrûmāh (offering "raised up" before God) came from the entire community. Men and women alike contributed: jewelry, linens, skills, time. The list of materials is specific — gold, silver, bronze, blue/purple/scarlet yarns, fine linen, goat hair, rams' skins, acacia wood, spices, olive oil, onyx, gems. The Tabernacle embodied the entire community's sacrifice: when they worshipped within it, they worshipped within their own offered lives.

V.2-3: The Tabernacle construction instructions open with a Sabbath reminder — even building God's house must not violate God's rest. The phrase shabbāt shabbātôn (a Sabbath of complete Sabbath-ness) is the most emphatic form: total rest. The proximity of Tabernacle-building and Sabbath command makes a profound point: the rest that the Tabernacle's God embodies is not overridden by the urgency of sacred construction. The work of God must be done in the rhythm of God.

hist.historical: The willing-heart offering produced such abundance that Moses had to restrain the people from giving more (36:6–7). This detail — excess generosity requiring restraint — appears nowhere else in the Pentateuch and creates a deliberate contrast with the grudging, complaining spirit of the wilderness murmuring narratives. The participation of women as skilled spinners and weavers (v. 25–26) is consistent with textile production evidence from ancient Near Eastern archaeological sites, where women dominated the weaving industry.
hist.context: The giving described in vv.21–29 is one of the most beautiful passages in Exodus. Men and women bring gold jewelry — earrings, brooches, rings. Skilled women spin yarn. Leaders bring gems. Everyone gives what they have, and they give it willingly. The contrast with ch.32 is stark: the same gold earrings that went into the golden calf are now being given for the Tabernacle. The repentance of ch.32–34 has produced transformed generosity.

refs.note: "Each of you should give what you have decided in your heart to give, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver." Paul's principle mirrors the Tabernacle giving precisely.
The people gave too much and had to be restrained — the only offering drive in Scripture where the problem was excess generosity.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The offering of gold jewellery — clasps, earrings, rings, necklaces — mirrors the items given for the golden calf (32:2–3). Sarna notes the same materials redirected: the idolatry-gold becomes the worship-gold.

The voluntary nature of the tabernacle gifts is emphasised by two repeated phrases: 'everyone whose heart moved them' (v.21) and 'all the Israelite men and women who were willing' (v.29). Sarna notes the repetition establishes that the tabernacle is the covenant community's freewill offering — the counterpart to the commanded offerings of the cult.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The repeated 'whose heart moved them' and 'whose spirit prompted them' creates a rhythm of willing interiority. Alter notes the collective outpouring is individual at its source — each willing heart contributing to a communal act.

The women's weaving and spinning — linen and goat hair for the coverings — is specifically mentioned alongside the men's metalwork and wood carving. Alter notes the community's contribution to the tabernacle transcends gender division: the whole community participates in building the divine dwelling, each according to their skill and capacity.

Calvin: 'Those who had abundance were ready to give; those who had nothing to give brought their labour; and thus all, according to their means, co-operated in the work.' The tabernacle is a communal project in which every contribution — material or labour — is equally honoured.

Calvin: The abundance of the offering reflects the transformation of the community's heart. The same people who gave their gold for the golden calf now give more than is needed for the legitimate sanctuary. Repentance is not merely negative (stopping the sin) but positive (redirecting the energy toward the right purpose.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'willing offerings to the LORD' explains that the term nedavah (freewill offering) appears throughout the Levitical system for offerings given beyond what is required. The tabernacle construction is itself structured as a nedavah — entirely voluntary.

The NET note on Bezalel and Oholiab's reintroduction in vv.30-35 traces the parallel between the Spirit-filling in ch.31 and its restatement here. The note discusses why both the command and the craftsmen's appointment are repeated: the repeated disclosure ensures the community knows who has divine authority for the construction.

sections.header: Verses 30–35 — Bezalel and Oholiab Announced; Spirit-Gifted Teachers

heb.paragraph: V.26: "All the women who were willing and had the skill spun the goat hair." The Tabernacle was a community product — artisans, craftswomen, metalworkers, embroiderers, donors. No single figure (except Bezalel as master-craftsman) is elevated. The corporate nature of sacred construction is itself a theological statement: the house of God belongs to the people of God, built by all of them together.

V.27-28: The leaders (nĕśîʾîm) brought the onyx and other gems for the priestly garments and the spices and oils for anointing and incense. Their special contribution — the most precious materials — reflects a tithe of leadership: those who lead give most. The pattern recurs throughout Scripture: the greatest gift comes from the most prominent position (cf. Luke 21:1-4 where the widow's offering exceeds all, and John 19:39 where Nicodemus brings 75 pounds of spices).

hist.historical: Bezalel and Oholiab are reintroduced with the added note that they are gifted not only with skill but with the ability to teach (v. 34). The concept of divinely gifted craftsmanship has parallels in Mesopotamian traditions where the god Ea/Enki was patron of craftsmen and bestowed skill (nēmequ) on chosen artisans. The Ugaritic texts similarly describe the craftsman-god Kothar-wa-Khasis as the divine architect. Israel's distinctive contribution is that the Spirit of God — not a craft-deity — empowers human artistry.
hist.context: The announcement of Bezalel and Oholiab (vv.30–35) answers the question the offering raises: the materials are now here; who has the skill to use them? God has already provided the answer — Spirit-filled craftsmen, equipped before the project began. This is the pattern of divine provision: the resources and the skills arrive together, both given by God, both freely offered by the community.

refs.note: Bezalel and Oholiab were first named in 31:1–6. Their reintroduction here after the golden calf narrative carries theological weight: the Spirit-gifted artisans are still called, still equipped. God's creative purposes endure.

Paul's theology of spiritual gifts — 'different kinds of gifts, but the same Spirit' — has its Old Testament root here: Bezalel is filled with the Spirit for artistic craftsmanship. Skill in the service of God is a charism.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The naming of Bezalel (in the shadow of God) alongside his Spirit-filling establishes a theology of Spirit-enabled craftsmanship. Sarna notes that the filling with divine Spirit (ru'ach Elohim) for craftwork is as theologically significant as its use for prophetic inspiration -- both are expressions of the same divine empowerment.

God gave Bezalel and Oholiab the ability to teach others. Sarna notes the master craftsman's responsibility to transmit skill is itself a divine gift. The tabernacle is not built by two geniuses but by a Spirit-equipped teaching community.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Bezalel and Oholiab are introduced as a pair -- master craftsman and assistant, from different tribes (Judah and Dan). Alter notes the pairing is deliberate: the tabernacle is a community project with a community leadership structure, not a solo genius achievement.

The list of Bezalel's competencies -- wisdom, understanding, knowledge, and all kinds of craftsmanship, to make artistic designs for work in gold, silver, and bronze -- is the most comprehensive inventory of skilled capacities given to any individual in the Pentateuch.

Calvin: The Spirit of God is given for craftsmanship as well as for prophecy. This teaches that all legitimate skills and arts come from God and that their exercise in his service is worship. The craftsman who builds the sanctuary with skill serves God as truly as the priest who offers sacrifice in it.

Calvin on the gift of teaching: God gave them ability to teach. The transmission of skill is itself a divine gift -- the master craftsman who teaches the community multiplies the Spirit-given capacity throughout the whole people. Sacred craft is never purely individual.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on chokmah (wisdom), tevunah (understanding), and da'at (knowledge) as Bezalel's Spirit-given qualifications discusses how these three terms together describe comprehensive intellectual and creative capacity. The same three terms appear in Prov 3:19-20 for God's own wisdom in creation -- the craftsman shares in the divine creative mode.

The NET note on the ability to teach (la'horot natan belibbo) discusses how God placed teaching ability in their hearts. The divine equipping is not merely technical but pedagogical -- the Spirit's gift includes the capacity to transmit what was received.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"Everyone whose heart stirred him, and everyone whose spirit moved him, came and brought the Lord's contribution."NIV"Everyone who was willing and whose heart moved them came and brought an offering to the Lord."NASB"Everyone whose heart stirred him came, and everyone whose spirit impelled him brought the Lord's contribution."

src.title: Voluntary Temple Offerings
src.quote: Ancient Near Eastern temple building was typically funded by royal decree and corvée labor. Israel's freewill offering model is exceptional in the ancient world — a whole community voluntarily funding a sacred project.
src.note: The willing-heart model is theologically and historically distinctive.

src.title: Women's Participation
src.quote: The spinning women of vv.25–26 represent significant female participation in the Tabernacle project. Their skilled work (spinning yarn) is explicitly honored as a contribution.
src.note: The Tabernacle project is notable for the explicit inclusion of women's contributions.

rec.text: Paul's theology of giving draws directly on the Tabernacle model: "whoever sows generously will also reap generously... God loves a cheerful giver."

David's prayer at the Temple offering: "Who am I, and who are my people, that we should be able to give as generously as this? Everything comes from you, and we have given you only what comes from your hand." The Tabernacle giving posture continued in Temple building.

rows.label: Willing Hearts: The Tabernacle as Covenant Community Offering
rows.text: Everyone whose heart moved them and everyone whose spirit prompted them came and brought an offering to the LORD. The tabernacle is built not by mandatory tribute but by voluntary response. The community's generosity funds the covenant dwelling.

rows.label: Women and Men: The Whole Community Participates
rows.text: The women who had skill spun the goat hair; the women who were willing brought gold and silver. The entire covenant community -- men and women, craftsmen and contributors -- participates in building the divine dwelling.

tx.title: Construction narrative — LXX's major rearrangement
tx.content: The LXX rearranges the construction account (chapters 35–40) significantly compared to MT. It follows a different order for describing the construction of tabernacle components, omits some repetitive material, and adds unique readings. This is the single largest structural divergence between MT and LXX in the Pentateuch.
tx.note: The rearrangement suggests the LXX translators had access to a different Hebrew Vorlage for the construction narrative, or that they editorially reshaped the material. 4QpaleoExodᵐ shows some affinity with the LXX order.

debate.title: The Willing Heart: Covenant Response or Natural Impulse?

positions.name: Covenant response to grace
positions.argument: The willing heart is the fruit of the Spirit's work in response to covenant grace already received. Israel gives freely because God gave first — the Exodus, Sinai, forgiveness after the golden calf.

positions.name: Natural religious community dynamics
positions.argument: The outpouring of gifts reflects the social and religious dynamics of a community with a shared project. Large voluntary communal gifts are a feature of intense religious community life.

coaching.tip: The tabernacle instructions repeat—now as execution. What God commanded (chs. 25-31), Israel builds (chs. 35-40). Obedience mirrors revelation. The echo structure says: they did exactly as told.

'Everyone who was willing and whose heart moved them' (v. 21). Offerings for the tabernacle are voluntary—no compulsion. Generosity flows from hearts, not quotas. Worship flourishes on willing sacrifice.
